perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, "Proprietary/Confidential Information" shall mean any and all proprietary, secret information, technical data or know-how related to any aspect of other party's business or technology including, without limitation, data, know- how, formulas, designs, photographs, drawings, specification, software programs and samples and any other material bearing or incorporating any such information which is disclosed by one party to the other, which information, data or know-how is marked or stipulated as being "Proprietary", "Confidential", "Strictly Private" or otherwise, using words of similar significance.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, "Proprietary/Confidential Information" shall mean only information explicitly marked as 'Proprietary' in bold, uppercase letters. No other form of marking or stipulation will be considered sufficient to designate information as proprietary or confidential under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly narrows the definition of 'Proprietary/Confidential Information,' contradicting standard trade secret law which protects unmarked information under certain circumstances. The original definition allows for broader interpretation through words 'of similar significance,' while the modification imposes a strict, narrow standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, which include information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. This modified definition removes the 'reasonable efforts' standard by requiring explicit marking, potentially nullifying protection for unmarked but genuinely confidential information that meets the legal standard of trade secret.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by significantly narrowing the definition of 'Proprietary/Confidential Information'. The original text allows for broader interpretation, including information 'marked or stipulated' as proprietary 'or otherwise, using words of similar significance.' The changed text requires information to be 'explicitly marked as 'Proprietary' in bold, uppercase letters.' This creates a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, because it deviates from legally recognized definitions of trade secrets. The Law Explanation describes how CUTSA protects trade secrets and requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, and the change removes the 'reasonable efforts' standard.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of 5 years from the date of this Agreement, the receiving party shall not publish, disseminate nor disclose any Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties to any third party accept to those of its own employment having valid need to know the information in the course of employment and such disclosure shall be on terms not less restrictive than those herein contained.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of 5 years from the date of this Agreement, the receiving party shall not publish, disseminate nor disclose any Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties to any third party, including those of its own employment, even if they have a valid need to know the information in the course of employment.
perturbation.explanation: This change restricts disclosure even to employees with a 'need to know,' potentially violating the 'employee mobility doctrine' which protects an employee's ability to use general knowledge gained during employment, and reasonable efforts to share such knowledge within the business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ipswitch, Inc. v. Innovative Network Solutions, Inc.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the employee mobility doctrine, which permits employees to utilize general knowledge and skills acquired during previous employment, including information not rising to the level of trade secrets. By restricting disclosure even to employees with a legitimate need to know, the clause potentially hinders employee mobility and the normal operation of the receiving party's business.

perturbation.justification: The change explicitly restricts disclosure even to employees with a 'need to know,' potentially conflicting with the employee mobility doctrine as described in the Perturbation and Law explanations. The original text allowed for disclosure to employees with a need to know, subject to confidentiality terms. The altered text removes this allowance, creating a potential contradiction. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not relevant, the core issue lies in the restriction of information flow within the company, potentially hindering the employee's ability to utilize general knowledge acquired during employment, which is an aspect covered under the employee mobility doctrine mentioned in the Law and Perturbation Explanations. This deviation from accepted practices creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Proprietary/Confidential Information as it uses in respect of its own information of like importance but in no case less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall use whatever degree of care it deems necessary to avoid disclosure or use of the Proprietary/Confidential Information, regardless of the care it uses for its own information, and no particular standard of care is required.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the 'reasonable degree of care' standard, contradicting the implicit duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts and potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' criteria for trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By stating 'no particular standard of care is required,' the changed text removes the reasonable degree of care, allowing for arbitrary or negligent handling of confidential information which undermines the contractual purpose and violates good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the requirement for a 'reasonable degree of care' in protecting confidential information, replacing it with 'whatever degree of care it deems necessary' and stating 'no particular standard of care is required.' This directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, and the definition of good faith in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which encompasses 'observance of fair dealing standards.' By allowing for potentially negligent handling of confidential information, the modified text undermines the contractual purpose and violates the principle of good faith, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Proprietary/Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Proprietary/Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement shall become the joint property of both the disclosing party and the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the ownership of the confidential information. Transferring ownership rights contradicts the purpose of a confidentiality agreement, and raises concerns regarding trade secret laws, as shared ownership can jeopardize trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, a trade secret's owner has the right to protect it from misappropriation. Changing the contract to imply joint ownership can compromise the disclosing party's ability to maintain the trade secret's exclusivity and take action against misuse, because any co-owner could disclose or use it. This undermines the protection intended by trade secret law and the original confidentiality agreement.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts trade secret law by altering the ownership of confidential information. The original text states that all proprietary information remains the property of the disclosing party, while the changed text makes it the joint property of both parties. This contradicts the spirit of trade secret protection, as exclusivity is a key component. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' and implies that the owner of a trade secret has the right to protect it. By making it joint property, the disclosing party's ability to prevent misappropriation is significantly weakened because the receiving party, as a co-owner, would have rights to disclose or use the information. This undermines the original intent to maintain secrecy and exclusivity, creating a direct legal contradiction.
